DECISION.description: A deliberate choice made between alternatives — includes rationale, what was chosen, and what was ruled out.
DECISION.example: Chose JWT over session cookies for the auth layer because the API is stateless and needs to support mobile clients.
DECISION.exclusions: Routine actions (closing a ticket, merging a PR), status updates with no choice made, observations without a decision point.

LEARNING.description: A new understanding, technique, or fact acquired — something that changes how you would approach a problem.
LEARNING.example: Discovered that chokidar's ignoreInitial option prevents spurious events on startup when watching config directories.
LEARNING.exclusions: Restatements of already-known facts, unverified claims, debugging steps that led nowhere.

PREFERENCE.description: A recurring aesthetic, stylistic, or workflow preference that should inform future work.
PREFERENCE.example: Prefers plain-language commit messages in imperative mood; avoids jargon-heavy summaries that obscure intent.
PREFERENCE.exclusions: One-time situational choices, preferences that contradict already-logged entries without explicit revision.

RELATIONSHIP.description: Context about a person — how they work, what they value, communication style, or relationship dynamics.
RELATIONSHIP.example: Sarah from recruiting responds quickly to concise bullets; prefers a headline summary before any detail.
RELATIONSHIP.exclusions: ISPN, Genesys, or Asana personnel. Generic impressions with no actionable context. Third-party quotes without interpretation.

CONSTRAINT.description: A hard boundary, rule, or non-negotiable requirement — architectural, organizational, legal, or personal.
CONSTRAINT.example: LEFT side of the vault is write-protected for agents; any file whose words should sound like Pete lives on the LEFT.
CONSTRAINT.exclusions: Soft preferences logged as PREFERENCE instead. Expired constraints (mark superseded before archiving).

PATTERN.description: A recurring solution or approach that has proven effective across multiple contexts.
PATTERN.example: When routing ambiguous input through a confidence gate, always fail LEFT (to proposals queue) rather than fail RIGHT — recoverable over irrecoverable.
PATTERN.exclusions: One-time solutions. Anti-patterns (log under CONSTRAINT instead). Patterns not yet validated across two or more occurrences.

OTHER.description: Information that is genuinely worth retaining but does not fit DECISION, LEARNING, PREFERENCE, RELATIONSHIP, CONSTRAINT, or PATTERN. Requires a one-sentence justification explaining why it is memorable and what future use it serves.
OTHER.example: The Obsidian Local REST API plugin listens on port 27123 by default. (Justification: saves a config lookup on every new MCP setup.)
OTHER.exclusions: Anything that clearly fits another category — use OTHER only after ruling out all six named categories. Do not use OTHER to avoid categorization effort. Justification is mandatory.
